FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=9,718, baseline data release 2.0.1, 21 recruitment sites, ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning (stacked ensemble) and follow-up LME analyses of ABCD baseline data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Child Development; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Follow-up LME analyses in the Results section report separate F-statistics for individual screen-time modalities against fluid cognitive functioning (FCF): social media use F(1,9662)=6.79, p<.01; time spent texting F(1,9666)=7.99, p<.005; video chatting F(1,9666)=6.81, p<.01. These are phone/SM-specific variables paired with FCF (a cognitive/neural individual-trait outcome). Step 1 satisfied; stop.

FT_IC3.evidence: F-statistics with p-values reported for social media, texting, and video chat versus FCF in LME follow-up analyses (e.g., social media F(1,9662)=6.79, p<.01).

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results present: F-statistics, p-values, variable importance scores, and Spearman r values reported throughout Tables 2 and Figure 1.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (authors state 'results are cross-sectional'); EC7 requires longitudinal design. Does not apply.
notes: The headline ML analysis uses a composite 'screen media activity weekday (STQ)' that mixes SM, texting, TV, videos, video games, R-rated movies, etc. However, Step 1 is satisfied by separate per-modality LME estimates (social media, texting, video chat vs. FCF) reported in the follow-up analyses section.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study prospective cohort, N=11,025, 21 U.S. sites, ages 9-10 baseline (2016-2018), ABCD 3.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: logistic regression of screen time modalities at baseline on binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Int J Eating Disorders; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports per-modality adjusted ORs against binge-eating disorder: social networking aOR 1.62 (95% CI 1.18-2.22, p=.003); texting aOR 1.40 (95% CI 1.08-1.82, p=.011); video chat aOR 1.32 (95% CI 0.99-1.76, p=.057). Social networking and texting are phone/SM-specific variables; binge-eating disorder is a clinical/health individual-trait outcome. Step 1 satisfied; stop.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted ORs with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for all screen-time modalities versus binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results present: aOR values, 95% CIs, and p-values for all modalities in Table 2.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables are IV (exposures) predicting binge-eating disorder (DV); EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis. Does not apply.
notes: Longitudinal prospective cohort (baseline to 1-year follow-up). Screen time modalities (including social networking and texting) as IV; binge-eating disorder as DV. Clear IC2 pass at Step 1 via per-modality estimates.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study prospective cohort, N=11,066, 21 U.S. sites, ages 9-10 baseline (2016-2018), ABCD 3.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multiple linear regression of screen-time modalities at baseline on BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Pediatric Obesity; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports per-modality B coefficients against BMI percentile: texting B=0.92 (95% CI 0.29-1.55, p=.004); video chat B=0.72 (95% CI 0.09-1.36, p=.025); social networking B=0.67 (95% CI -0.07-1.41, p=.075). Texting and video chat are phone/SM-specific variables paired with BMI percentile (a biological/physical-health individual-trait outcome). Step 1 satisfied; stop.

FT_IC3.evidence: B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values reported for all screen-time modalities versus BMI percentile in Table 2 (fully adjusted model).

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results present: B coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values for all modalities in Table 2; sensitivity analyses with BMI and BMI z-score in appendices.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables are IV (exposures) predicting BMI percentile (DV); EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis. Does not apply.
notes: Longitudinal prospective cohort (baseline to 1-year follow-up). Screen time modalities (including texting, video chat, social networking) as IV; BMI percentile as DV. Clear IC2 pass at Step 1 via per-modality estimates.
